{{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of {{ill|Nishinomiya Shrine|ja|西宮神社}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}. Known since the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it is ideal for brewing {{ill|Japanese sake|ja|日本酒}} and indispensable for the {{ill|Nada Gogō|ja|灘五郷}}, a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗}}, in {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of {{ill|subsurface water|ja|伏流水}} from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川}} river and water percolating through the {{ill|granite|ja|花崗岩}} of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山}}, combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as {{ill|calcium|ja|カルシウム}} and {{ill|potassium|ja|カリウム}}, which serve as nutrients for {{ill|kōji|ja|麹}} and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the {{ill|Hanshin Industrial Zone|ja|阪神工業地帯}}. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the {{ill|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line|ja|阪神高速3号神戸線}} near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

{{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of {{ill|Nishinomiya Shrine|ja|西宮神社}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}. Known since the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it is ideal for brewing {{ill|Japanese sake|ja|日本酒}} and indispensable for the {{ill|Nada Gogō|ja|灘五郷}}, a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗}}, in {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of {{ill|subsurface water|ja|伏流水}} from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川}} river and water percolating through the {{ill|granite|ja|花崗岩}} of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山}}, combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as {{ill|calcium|ja|カルシウム}} and {{ill|potassium|ja|カリウム}}, which serve as nutrients for {{ill|kōji|ja|麹}} and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the {{ill|Hanshin Industrial Zone|ja|阪神工業地帯}}. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the {{ill|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line|ja|阪神高速3号神戸線}} near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

{{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of {{ill|Nishinomiya Shrine|ja|西宮神社}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}. Known since the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it is ideal for brewing {{ill|Japanese sake|ja|日本酒}} and indispensable for the {{ill|Nada Gogō|ja|灘五郷}}, a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗}}, in {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of {{ill|subsurface water|ja|伏流水}} from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川}} river and water percolating through the {{ill|granite|ja|花崗岩}} of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山}}, combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as {{ill|calcium|ja|カルシウム}} and {{ill|potassium|ja|カリウム}}, which serve as nutrients for {{ill|kōji|ja|麹}} and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the {{ill|Hanshin Industrial Zone|ja|阪神工業地帯}}. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the {{ill|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line|ja|阪神高速3号神戸線}} near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Nada Gogō#Historical development|ja|灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで}} * {{ill|Nada Gogō|ja|灘五郷}} * {{ill|Kudari-zake|ja|下り酒}} * {{ill|History of sake|ja|日本酒の歴史#江戸後期}} * {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}} * {{ill|Groundwater|ja|地下水}}
== External Links == * [https://water-pub.env.go.jp/water-pub/mizu-site/meisui/data/index.asp?info=57 Ministry of the Environment - Miyamizu (100 Exquisite Waters)] * [http://kobe.travel.coocan.jp/nishinomiya/miyamizu.htm Miyamizu (Famous Water of Nishinomiya) - Umenoki Well photos] * [http://sakenomuzo.web.fc2.com/ Sakenomuzo's Website - Miyamizu details]
{{100 Exquisite Waters}}

== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of {{ill|subsurface water|ja|伏流水}} from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川}} river and water percolating through the {{ill|granite|ja|花崗岩}} of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山}}, combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as {{ill|calcium|ja|カルシウム}} and {{ill|potassium|ja|カリウム}}, which serve as nutrients for {{ill|kōji|ja|麹}} and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the {{ill|Hanshin Industrial Zone|ja|阪神工業地帯}}. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the {{ill|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line|ja|阪神高速3号神戸線}} near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

{{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of {{ill|Nishinomiya Shrine|ja|西宮神社}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}. Known since the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it is ideal for brewing {{ill|Japanese sake|ja|日本酒}} and indispensable for the {{ill|Nada Gogō|ja|灘五郷}}, a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗}}, in {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of {{ill|subsurface water|ja|伏流水}} from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川}} river and water percolating through the {{ill|granite|ja|花崗岩}} of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山}}, combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as {{ill|calcium|ja|カルシウム}} and {{ill|potassium|ja|カリウム}}, which serve as nutrients for {{ill|kōji|ja|麹}} and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the {{ill|Hanshin Industrial Zone|ja|阪神工業地帯}}. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the {{ill|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line|ja|阪神高速3号神戸線}} near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of {{ill|Nishinomiya Shrine|ja|西宮神社}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}. Known since the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it is ideal for brewing {{ill|Japanese sake|ja|日本酒}} and indispensable for the {{ill|Nada Gogō|ja|灘五郷}}, a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗}}, in {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of {{ill|subsurface water|ja|伏流水}} from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川}} river and water percolating through the {{ill|granite|ja|花崗岩}} of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山}}, combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as {{ill|calcium|ja|カルシウム}} and {{ill|potassium|ja|カリウム}}, which serve as nutrients for {{ill|kōji|ja|麹}} and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the {{ill|Hanshin Industrial Zone|ja|阪神工業地帯}}. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the {{ill|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line|ja|阪神高速3号神戸線}} near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Nada Gogō#Historical development|ja|灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで}} * {{ill|Nada Gogō|ja|灘五郷}} * {{ill|Kudari-zake|ja|下り酒}} * {{ill|History of sake|ja|日本酒の歴史#江戸後期}} * {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}} * {{ill|Groundwater|ja|地下水}}
== External Links == * [https://water-pub.env.go.jp/water-pub/mizu-site/meisui/data/index.asp?info=57 Ministry of the Environment - Miyamizu (100 Exquisite Waters)] * [http://kobe.travel.coocan.jp/nishinomiya/miyamizu.htm Miyamizu (Famous Water of Nishinomiya) - Umenoki Well photos] * [http://sakenomuzo.web.fc2.com/ Sakenomuzo's Website - Miyamizu details]
{{100 Exquisite Waters}}

For instance, construction of the {{ill|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line|ja|阪神高速3号神戸線}} near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of {{ill|Nishinomiya Shrine|ja|西宮神社}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}. Known since the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it is ideal for brewing {{ill|Japanese sake|ja|日本酒}} and indispensable for the {{ill|Nada Gogō|ja|灘五郷}}, a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗}}, in {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of {{ill|subsurface water|ja|伏流水}} from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川}} river and water percolating through the {{ill|granite|ja|花崗岩}} of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山}}, combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as {{ill|calcium|ja|カルシウム}} and {{ill|potassium|ja|カリウム}}, which serve as nutrients for {{ill|kōji|ja|麹}} and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the {{ill|Hanshin Industrial Zone|ja|阪神工業地帯}}. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the {{ill|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line|ja|阪神高速3号神戸線}} near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Nada Gogō#Historical development|ja|灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで}} * {{ill|Nada Gogō|ja|灘五郷}} * {{ill|Kudari-zake|ja|下り酒}} * {{ill|History of sake|ja|日本酒の歴史#江戸後期}} * {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}} * {{ill|Groundwater|ja|地下水}}
== External Links == * [https://water-pub.env.go.jp/water-pub/mizu-site/meisui/data/index.asp?info=57 Ministry of the Environment - Miyamizu (100 Exquisite Waters)] * [http://kobe.travel.coocan.jp/nishinomiya/miyamizu.htm Miyamizu (Famous Water of Nishinomiya) - Umenoki Well photos] * [http://sakenomuzo.web.fc2.com/ Sakenomuzo's Website - Miyamizu details]

For instance, construction of the {{ill|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line|ja|阪神高速3号神戸線}} near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of {{ill|Nishinomiya Shrine|ja|西宮神社}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}. Known since the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it is ideal for brewing {{ill|Japanese sake|ja|日本酒}} and indispensable for the {{ill|Nada Gogō|ja|灘五郷}}, a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗}}, in {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of {{ill|subsurface water|ja|伏流水}} from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川}} river and water percolating through the {{ill|granite|ja|花崗岩}} of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山}}, combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as {{ill|calcium|ja|カルシウム}} and {{ill|potassium|ja|カリウム}}, which serve as nutrients for {{ill|kōji|ja|麹}} and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the {{ill|Hanshin Industrial Zone|ja|阪神工業地帯}}. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the {{ill|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line|ja|阪神高速3号神戸線}} near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Nada Gogō#Historical development|ja|灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで}} * {{ill|Nada Gogō|ja|灘五郷}} * {{ill|Kudari-zake|ja|下り酒}} * {{ill|History of sake|ja|日本酒の歴史#江戸後期}} * {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}} * {{ill|Groundwater|ja|地下水}}
== External Links == * [https://water-pub.env.go.jp/water-pub/mizu-site/meisui/data/index.asp?info=57 Ministry of the Environment - Miyamizu (100 Exquisite Waters)] * [http://kobe.travel.coocan.jp/nishinomiya/miyamizu.htm Miyamizu (Famous Water of Nishinomiya) - Umenoki Well photos] * [http://sakenomuzo.web.fc2.com/ Sakenomuzo's Website - Miyamizu details]

For instance, construction of the {{ill|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line|ja|阪神高速3号神戸線}} near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also ==" to " == See Also! =="
revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of {{ill|Nishinomiya Shrine|ja|西宮神社}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}. Known since the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it is ideal for brewing {{ill|Japanese sake|ja|日本酒}} and indispensable for the {{ill|Nada Gogō|ja|灘五郷}}, a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗}}, in {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of {{ill|subsurface water|ja|伏流水}} from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川}} river and water percolating through the {{ill|granite|ja|花崗岩}} of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山}}, combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as {{ill|calcium|ja|カルシウム}} and {{ill|potassium|ja|カリウム}}, which serve as nutrients for {{ill|kōji|ja|麹}} and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the {{ill|Hanshin Industrial Zone|ja|阪神工業地帯}}. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the {{ill|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line|ja|阪神高速3号神戸線}} near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

== External Links == * [https://water-pub.env.go.jp/water-pub/mizu-site/meisui/data/index.asp?info=57 Ministry of the Environment - Miyamizu (100 Exquisite Waters)] * [http://kobe.travel.coocan.jp/nishinomiya/miyamizu.htm Miyamizu (Famous Water of Nishinomiya) - Umenoki Well photos] * [http://sakenomuzo.web.fc2.com/ Sakenomuzo's Website - Miyamizu details]

Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the {{ill|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line|ja|阪神高速3号神戸線}} near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Nada Gogō#Historical development|ja|灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで}} * {{ill|Nada Gogō|ja|灘五郷}} * {{ill|Kudari-zake|ja|下り酒}} * {{ill|History of sake|ja|日本酒の歴史#江戸後期}} * {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選}} * {{ill|Groundwater|ja|地下水}}
== External Links == * [https://water-pub.env.go.jp/water-pub/mizu-site/meisui/data/index.asp?info=57 Ministry of the Environment - Miyamizu (100 Exquisite Waters)] * [http://kobe.travel.coocan.jp/nishinomiya/miyamizu.htm Miyamizu (Famous Water of Nishinomiya) - Umenoki Well photos] * [http://sakenomuzo.web.fc2.com/ Sakenomuzo's Website - Miyamizu details]

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月}} {{独自研究|date=2017年8月}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|西宮市久保町にある宮水発祥の地の碑、周辺には有名酒造会社の井戸が立ち並ぶ]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|明治時代の酒蔵の井戸]] '''宮水'''（みやみず）とは、今の[[兵庫県]][[西宮市]]の[[西宮神社]]の南東側一帯から湧出する、[[日本酒]]つくりに適していると[[江戸時代]]後期から知られている[[水]]。[[灘五郷]]の酒造に欠かせない名水として知られる。[[硬度 (水)|硬度]]が高く、カリウムやリンの含有量が多く、鉄分が少ない特徴がある<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=宮水保全条例について |url=https://www.nishi.or.jp/bunka/kanko/jyourei/miyamizuhozen.html |website=www.nishi.or.jp |access-date=2025-03-08 |language=ja |publisher=[[西宮市]]}}</ref>。宮水を守るため、水質保全活動が以前からなされている。
== 由来 == [[天保]]8年（[[1837年]]）、一説には天保11年（[[1840年]]）、[[櫻正宗]]の六代目[[蔵元]]であった[[山邑太左衛門]]（やまむらたざえもん）が[[摂津国]]西宮（現兵庫県西宮市）で発見したとされる<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=入江 経明|date=1992-01|title=おいしい酒 : 灘の宮水|journal=ファルマシア|volume=28|issue=1|pages=33 - 34|publisher=[[日本薬学会]]|ref=harv|doi=10.14894/faruawpsj.28.1_33}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=環境省選定 名水百選／詳細ページ |url=https://water-pub.env.go.jp/water-pub/mizu-site/meisui/data/index.asp?info=57 |website=water-pub.env.go.jp |access-date=2025-03-08 |publisher=[[環境省]]}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=灘五郷:風土 |url=https://www.nadagogo.ne.jp/climate/ |website=www.nadagogo.ne.jp |access-date=2025-03-08 |publisher=灘五郷酒造組合}}</ref>。[[ヨドコウ迎賓館|山邑]]太左衛門は西宮と[[魚崎]]（現[[神戸市]][[東灘区]]）で[[造り酒屋]]を営んでいたが、双方で造る酒は味が異なった<ref name=":2" />。西宮で造る酒の良質な味の原因について、彼は「同地にある梅ノ木蔵の「梅ノ木井戸」の水にある」と結論づけた<ref name=":2" />。これが「宮水の発見」とされる<ref name=":2" />。
当初、｢'''西宮の水'''」と言っていたが、やがて略されて「'''宮水'''」と呼ばれるようになった<ref name=":1" />。
以後、灘の[[酒蔵]]は競ってこの地の水を使うようになったが、井戸を掘っても同じ水脈に当たらない酒蔵もあった。そのため、地域の農民らが井戸を掘り、酒蔵に宮水を売るようにもなった。西宮に特有のこの商売をさして「水屋」といった<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html |title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋 |publisher=神戸市文書館 |accessdate=2019-03-11 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html |title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集 |publisher=灘酒研究会 |accessdate=2019-03-11 }}</ref>。
== 成分 == かつて海だった地域を流れる法安寺伏流と札場筋伏流、夙川を起源とする戎伏流の3つの水流が合流して湧出する<ref name=":0" />。法安寺伏流と札場筋伏流は、カリウム、リンなどを多く含む一方で、戎伏流は酸素を多く含み、水中の鉄分が酸化鉄となるため、鉄分の含有量が少ない<ref name=":0" />。これらの伏流水が合流することで、ミネラルが豊富で鉄分が少ない宮水が形成される<ref name=":0" />。水質は、[[硬度 (水)|ドイツ硬度]]で8°dH前後の[[中硬水]]で、軟水の多い近畿地方では珍しい（日本国内では[[関東地方]]の水に近い硬度である）。
宮水に多く含まれるカリウムや[[リン]]は、[[麹]]や[[酵母]]の栄養分となり、[[酵素]]の作用を促進する<ref name=":0" />。また、酒造りの水には少量の塩分の含有が好まれるが、宮水は塩分も多い。逆に、酒造りに害となる鉄分は、宮水では0.001ppmと少ない（鉄分は酒の色や味の仕上がりを損なう。一般的に日本の水の[[鉄分]]含有量はだいたい0.02[[ppm]]程度）。
== 保全活動 == [[幕末]]以後、宮水は[[昭和時代]]初期までに「[[播州米]]に宮水、[[丹波杜氏]]に[[六甲山#六甲颪|六甲颪]]（ろっこうおろし）、[[男酒]]の[[灘五郷|灘の生一本]]」の名声を得る上で欠かせない原料となった。しかし、昭和中期以降に高度経済成長の時代を迎え、西宮も[[阪神工業地帯]]の真っ只中にあることから宮水の汚染が危惧された。この時期の何回かの調査で水質の汚濁があったことも判明し、酒郷組合等は宮水保全のための努力を継続して行ってきた。
例えば、[[阪神高速3号神戸線]]の[[橋脚]]は宮水地帯付近では間隔が極めて広く取られたほか、[[阪神電気鉄道|阪神]][[西宮駅 (阪神)|西宮駅]]高架化の際にも影響がないかを確認するために少しずつ工事を進めては水質に影響が出ていないか確認を繰り返したため、着工から完成まで20年以上も要する<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2 |title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは |newspaper=[[週刊ポスト|NEWSポストセブン]] |publisher=[[小学館]] |date=2019-09-23 |accessdate=2019-10-07 }}</ref>など、宮水の保全と産業発展の両立が図られている。
灘五郷酒造組合は水資源委員会を設置し、西宮市は宮水保全条例を制定するなどの保全活動も行われている<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=酒造用地下水（宮水）の保全に関する対応の件 |url=https://www.nadagogo.ne.jp/about/miyamizu/ |website=www.nadagogo.ne.jp |access-date=2025-03-08 |publisher=[[灘五郷酒造組合]]}}</ref>。その結果、阪神工業地帯の真ん中に位置するにもかかわらず、奇跡的な保全状態が保たれ、[[環境省]]の[[名水百選]]にも選ばれた<ref name=":1" />。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == <!-- 関連項目：本文記事を理解する上での補足として役立つ、関連性のある項目へのウィキ間リンク（姉妹プロジェクトリンク、言語間リンク）、ウィキリンク（ウィキペディア内部リンク）。可能なら本文内に埋め込んで下さい。 --> * [[灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで|灘目三郷]] * [[灘五郷]] * [[下り酒]]（くだりざけ） * [[日本酒の歴史#江戸後期]] * [[名水百選]] * [[地下水]]
== 外部リンク == * [https://water-pub.env.go.jp/water-pub/mizu-site/meisui/data/index.asp?info=57 環境省選定名水百選 - 宮水](環境省HP) * [http://kobe.travel.coocan.jp/nishinomiya/miyamizu.htm 宮水（西宮の名水）・宮水発祥の地「梅の木井戸」] - 写真あり * [http://sakenomuzo.web.fc2.com/ 鮭野夢造のホームページ]の[http://sakenomuzo.web.fc2.com/miyamizu.html （「新辛口批評 別冊 宮水の話」に解説されている）]
{{名水百選}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:みやみす}} [[Category:名水百選]] [[Category:西宮市]] [[Category:日本酒]] [[Category:灘五郷]] [[Category:兵庫県の酒]]

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyamizu|qq=}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. Known since the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it is ideal for brewing [[:en:Sake|Japanese sake]] and indispensable for the [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]], a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hardness|qq=}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Tazaemon VI|qq=}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramasamune|qq=}}, in [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinada-ku, Kobe|qq=}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|qq=}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|qq=}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|qq=}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamba Tōji|qq=}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|qq=}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|qq=}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Nada Gogō#Historical development|ja|灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Gogō#Historical development|qq=}} * [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]] * {{ill|Kudari-zake|ja|下り酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kudari-zake|qq=}} * {{ill|History of sake|ja|日本酒の歴史#江戸後期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of sake|qq=}} * {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|qq=}} * [[:en:Groundwater|Groundwater]]
== External Links == * [https://water-pub.env.go.jp/water-pub/mizu-site/meisui/data/index.asp?info=57 Ministry of the Environment - Miyamizu (100 Exquisite Waters)] * [http://kobe.travel.coocan.jp/nishinomiya/miyamizu.htm Miyamizu (Famous Water of Nishinomiya) - Umenoki Well photos] * [http://sakenomuzo.web.fc2.com/ Sakenomuzo's Website - Miyamizu details]

For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|qq=}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

[[sn:Miya Water]] [[en:Miya Water]] [[ja:宮水]]
<!--Miya Water--> [[Category:兵庫県の酒]] [[Category:名水百選]] [[Category:Sake]]

{{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyamizu|qq=}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. Known since the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it is ideal for brewing [[:en:Sake|Japanese sake]] and indispensable for the [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]], a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hardness|qq=}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Tazaemon VI|qq=}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramasamune|qq=}}, in [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinada-ku, Kobe|qq=}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|qq=}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|qq=}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|qq=}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamba Tōji|qq=}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|qq=}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|qq=}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinada-ku, Kobe|qq=}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|qq=}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|qq=}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|qq=}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamba Tōji|qq=}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|qq=}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|qq=}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

Known since the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it is ideal for brewing [[:en:Sake|Japanese sake]] and indispensable for the [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]], a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hardness|qq=}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.

Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|qq=}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|qq=}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamba Tōji|qq=}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.

These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>

In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamba Tōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|qq=}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Nada Gogō#Historical development|ja|灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Gogō#Historical development|qq=}} * [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]] * {{ill|Kudari-zake|ja|下り酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kudari-zake|qq=}} * {{ill|History of sake|ja|日本酒の歴史#江戸後期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of sake|qq=}} * {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|qq=}} * [[:en:Groundwater|Groundwater]]

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:兵庫県の酒]] into [[Category:Sake of Hyōgo Prefecture]] via bot
revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyamizu|qq=}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. Known since the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it is ideal for brewing [[:en:Sake|Japanese sake]] and indispensable for the [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]], a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hardness|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Tazaemon VI|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramasamune|qq=}}, in [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinada-ku, Kobe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|qq=}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tamba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamba Tōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|qq=}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Nada Gogō#Historical development|ja|灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Gogō#Historical development|qq=}} * [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]] * {{ill|Kudari-zake|ja|下り酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kudari-zake|qq=}} * {{ill|History of sake|ja|日本酒の歴史#江戸後期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of sake|qq=}} * {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|qq=}} * [[:en:Groundwater|Groundwater]]
== External Links == * [https://water-pub.env.go.jp/water-pub/mizu-site/meisui/data/index.asp?info=57 Ministry of the Environment - Miyamizu (100 Exquisite Waters)] * [http://kobe.travel.coocan.jp/nishinomiya/miyamizu.htm Miyamizu (Famous Water of Nishinomiya) - Umenoki Well photos] * [http://sakenomuzo.web.fc2.com/ Sakenomuzo's Website - Miyamizu details]

These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|qq=|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.
