<!--Miya Water--> [[Category:Sake of Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Japan's Top 100 Exquisite Waters]] [[Category:Sake]] [[Category:Nada-Gogō]] [[Category:Nishinomiya]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11454978]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyamizu|qq=|1=Miya Water|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11454978}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. Known since the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it is ideal for brewing [[:en:Sake|Japanese sake]] and indispensable for the [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]], a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hardness|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=water hardness|WD=Q192905}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Tazaemon VI|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramasamune|qq=|1=Sakuramasamune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538121}}, in [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinada-ku, Kobe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|qq=|1=Shukugawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430571}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和|1=Shōwa era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327567}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tanba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Tōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|qq=|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|qq=|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.
== References == {{Reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮水]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11454978]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyamizu|qq=|1=Miya Water|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11454978}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. Known since the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it is ideal for brewing [[:en:Sake|Japanese sake]] and indispensable for the [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]], a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hardness|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=water hardness|WD=Q192905}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Tazaemon VI|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramasamune|qq=|1=Sakuramasamune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538121}}, in [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinada-ku, Kobe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|qq=|1=Shukugawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430571}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和|1=Shōwa era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327567}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tanba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Tōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|qq=|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

== See Also == * {{ill|Nada Gogō#Historical development|ja|灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Gogō#Historical development|qq=|1=Nada-Gogō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6957369}} * [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]] * {{ill|Kudari-zake|ja|下り酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kudari-zake|qq=|1=Kudarizake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360567}} * {{ill|History of sake|ja|日本酒の歴史#江戸後期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of sake|qq=|1=history of sake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509280}} * {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|qq=|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}} * [[:en:Groundwater|Groundwater]]

This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|1=Shukugawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430571}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和|1=Shōwa era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327567}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tanba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Tōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮水]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11454978]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyamizu|1=Miya Water|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11454978}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. Known since the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it is ideal for brewing [[:en:Sake|Japanese sake]] and indispensable for the [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]], a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hardness|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=water hardness|WD=Q192905}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramasamune|1=Sakuramasamune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538121}}, in [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|1=Shukugawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430571}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和|1=Shōwa era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327567}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tanba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Tōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|1=Shukugawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430571}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和|1=Shōwa era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327567}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tanba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Tōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮水]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11454978]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyamizu|1=Miya Water|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11454978}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. Known since the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it is ideal for brewing [[:en:Sake|Japanese sake]] and indispensable for the [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]], a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hardness|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=water hardness|WD=Q192905}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramasamune|1=Sakuramasamune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538121}}, in [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>

Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和|1=Shōwa era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327567}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tanba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Tōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.

Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|1=Shukugawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430571}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和|1=Shōwa era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327567}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tanba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Tōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮水]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11454978]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyamizu|1=Miya Water|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11454978}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. Known since the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it is ideal for brewing [[:en:Sake|Japanese sake]] and indispensable for the [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]], a famed sake-producing region. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramasamune|1=Sakuramasamune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538121}}, in [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hardness|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=water hardness|WD=Q192905}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramasamune|1=Sakuramasamune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538121}}, in [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|1=Shukugawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430571}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和|1=Shōwa era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327567}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tanba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Tōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Nada Gogō#Historical development|ja|灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Gogō#Historical development|1=Nada-Gogō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6957369}} * [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]] * {{ill|Kudari-zake|ja|下り酒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kudari-zake|1=Kudarizake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360567}} * {{ill|History of sake|ja|日本酒の歴史#江戸後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of sake|1=history of sake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509280}} * {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}} * [[:en:Groundwater|Groundwater]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮水]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11454978]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyamizu|1=Miya Water|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11454978}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. Known since the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it is ideal for brewing [[:en:Sake|Japanese sake]] and indispensable for the [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]], a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hardness|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=water hardness|WD=Q192905}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramasamune|1=Sakuramasamune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538121}}, in [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|1=Shukugawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430571}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和|1=Shōwa era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327567}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tanba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Tōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Nada Gogō#Historical development|ja|灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Gogō#Historical development|1=Nada-Gogō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6957369}} * [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]] * {{ill|Kudari-zake|ja|下り酒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kudari-zake|1=Kudarizake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360567}} * {{ill|History of sake|ja|日本酒の歴史#江戸後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of sake|1=history of sake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509280}} * {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}} * [[:en:Groundwater|Groundwater]]
== External Links == * [https://water-pub.env.go.jp/water-pub/mizu-site/meisui/data/index.asp?info=57 Ministry of the Environment - Miyamizu (100 Exquisite Waters)] * [http://kobe.travel.coocan.jp/nishinomiya/miyamizu.htm Miyamizu (Famous Water of Nishinomiya) - Umenoki Well photos] * [http://sakenomuzo.web.fc2.com/ Sakenomuzo's Website - Miyamizu details]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮水]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮水]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11454978]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyamizu|1=Miya Water|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11454978}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. Known since the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it is ideal for brewing [[:en:Sake|Japanese sake]] and indispensable for the [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]], a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hardness|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=water hardness|WD=Q192905}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramasamune|1=Sakuramasamune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538121}}, in [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|1=Shukugawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430571}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和|1=Shōwa era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327567}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tanba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Tōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮水]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮水]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮水]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11454978]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyamizu|1=Miya Water|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11454978}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. Known since the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it is ideal for brewing [[:en:Sake|Japanese sake]] and indispensable for the [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]], a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hardness|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=water hardness|WD=Q192905}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramasamune|1=Sakuramasamune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538121}}, in [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|1=Shukugawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430571}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和|1=Shōwa era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327567}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tanba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Tōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Nada Gogō#Historical development|ja|灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Gogō#Historical development|1=Nada-Gogō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6957369}} * [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]] * {{ill|Kudari-zake|ja|下り酒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kudari-zake|1=Kudarizake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360567}} * {{ill|History of sake|ja|日本酒の歴史#江戸後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of sake|1=history of sake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509280}} * {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}} * [[:en:Groundwater|Groundwater]]
== External Links == * [https://water-pub.env.go.jp/water-pub/mizu-site/meisui/data/index.asp?info=57 Ministry of the Environment - Miyamizu (100 Exquisite Waters)] * [http://kobe.travel.coocan.jp/nishinomiya/miyamizu.htm Miyamizu (Famous Water of Nishinomiya) - Umenoki Well photos] * [http://sakenomuzo.web.fc2.com/ Sakenomuzo's Website - Miyamizu details]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮水]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮水]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宮水]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11454978]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2017}} {{Original research|date=August 2017}} [[File:Miyamizu monument 01.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of {{ill|Miyamizu|ja|宮水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyamizu|1=Miya Water|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11454978}} in Kubocho, Nishinomiya City, surrounded by wells of famous sake breweries]] [[File:Miyamizu Well in Meiji era.jpg|thumb|300px|A brewery well in the Meiji era]] '''Miyamizu''' (宮水) refers to the water that springs forth from the southeastern area of [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. Known since the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], it is ideal for brewing [[:en:Sake|Japanese sake]] and indispensable for the [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]], a famed sake-producing region. Miyamizu is distinguished by its high {{ill|hardness|ja|硬度 (水)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hardness|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=water hardness|WD=Q192905}} level, significant phosphorus content, and low iron concentration. Preservation efforts have long been in place to maintain its water quality.
== Origins == Miyamizu is said to have been discovered by {{ill|Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja|山邑太左衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Tazaemon VI|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sixth-generation brewer of {{ill|Sakuramasamune|ja|櫻正宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramasamune|1=Sakuramasamune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538121}}, in [[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 8 (1837) or, according to another theory, in Tenpō 11 (1840). Yamamura operated sake breweries in both Nishinomiya and Uozaki (present-day {{ill|Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市東灘区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinada-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), noticing that sake brewed in Nishinomiya was superior in flavor. He concluded that the superior taste was due to the water from the "Umenoki Well" in Nishinomiya, thus marking the discovery of Miyamizu.
Initially called "Nishinomiya Water," it eventually became known as "Miyamizu."
Subsequently, breweries in the Nada region began competing to use water from this area. However, not all breweries were successful in accessing the same aquifer, leading local farmers, who could dig wells yielding similar water, to sell Miyamizu to breweries. This distinctive trade in Nishinomiya was referred to as "Mizuya" (water dealers).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/document/syuzo/14.html|title=灘の酒造業、14 宮水と水屋|publisher=Kobe City Archives|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nada-ken.com/main/jp/index_mi/89.html|title=宮水{{!}}灘の酒用語集|publisher=Nada Sake Research Association|accessdate=2019-03-11}}</ref>
== Composition == Miyamizu is believed to be a mix of [[:en:Subterranean river|subsurface water]] from the nearby {{ill|Shukugawa|ja|夙川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukugawa|1=Shukugawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430571}} river and water percolating through the [[:en:Granite|granite]] of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], combined with traces of seawater. Its unique properties include a moderately hard water level (around 8°dH in German hardness), unusual for the typically soft water of the Kansai region (closer in hardness to water from the Kantō region).
Miyamizu contains abundant minerals such as [[:en:Calcium|calcium]] and [[:en:Potassium|potassium]], which serve as nutrients for [[:en:Kōji (food)|kōji]] and yeast, enhancing enzymatic activity. It also has a desirable salt content for sake brewing. In contrast, its iron content is extremely low at 0.001 ppm (iron typically hinders sake brewing by affecting flavor and color; Japanese water generally contains around 0.02 ppm of iron).
Despite modern scientific advances, some aspects of why Miyamizu is so uniquely suited for sake brewing remain unexplained.
== History == From the late [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period to the early {{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和|1=Shōwa era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327567}}, Miyamizu became a crucial ingredient in establishing the reputation of "Nada no Kiippon" (灘の生一本), the renowned sake of Nada, alongside {{ill|Banshū rice|ja|播州米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banshū rice|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the craftsmanship of {{ill|Tanba Tōji|ja|丹波杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Tōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the "Rokkō Oroshi" (六甲颪) winds from Mount Rokkō.
During Japan's {{ill|postwar economic miracle|ja|高度経済成長期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postwar economic miracle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高度経済成長|1=Japanese economic miracle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1059474}}, concerns about pollution arose as Nishinomiya became part of the [[:en:Hanshin Industrial Region|Hanshin Industrial Zone]]. Investigations revealed some water contamination, prompting preservation efforts led by sake guilds and associations. These efforts involved significant cooperation from both the government and businesses. For instance, construction of the [[:en:Kobe Route|Hanshin Expressway Route 3 Kobe Line]] near the Miyamizu region included design modifications to minimize impact, and the elevation of Nishinomiya Station required over 20 years to ensure water quality.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.news-postseven.com/archives/20190923_1454217.html/2|title=踏切事故で高まる立体交差化の要望 実現までの道のりは|newspaper=NEWSポストセブン|publisher=Shogakukan|date=2019-09-23|accessdate=2019-10-07}}</ref>
These preservation efforts have resulted in a "miraculous" maintenance of Miyamizu's quality, earning it a place among Japan's {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}}, designated by the Ministry of the Environment.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Nada Gogō#Historical development|ja|灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Gogō#Historical development|1=Nada-Gogō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6957369}} * [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]] * {{ill|Kudari-zake|ja|下り酒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kudari-zake|1=Kudarizake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360567}} * {{ill|History of sake|ja|日本酒の歴史#江戸後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of sake|1=history of sake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509280}} * {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}} * [[:en:Groundwater|Groundwater]]
== External Links == * [https://water-pub.env.go.jp/water-pub/mizu-site/meisui/data/index.asp?info=57 Ministry of the Environment - Miyamizu (100 Exquisite Waters)] * [http://kobe.travel.coocan.jp/nishinomiya/miyamizu.htm Miyamizu (Famous Water of Nishinomiya) - Umenoki Well photos] * [http://sakenomuzo.web.fc2.com/ Sakenomuzo's Website - Miyamizu details]

== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Nada Gogō#Historical development|ja|灘五郷#江戸時代前期まで|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Gogō#Historical development|1=Nada-Gogō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6957369}} * [[:en:Nada-Gogō|Nada Gogō]] * {{ill|Kudari-zake|ja|下り酒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kudari-zake|1=Kudarizake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360567}} * {{ill|History of sake|ja|日本酒の歴史#江戸後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of sake|1=history of sake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509280}} * {{ill|100 Exquisite Waters|ja|名水百選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/100 Exquisite Waters|1=Selected 100 Exquisite and Well-Conserved Waters of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917783}} * [[:en:Groundwater|Groundwater]]
